# Weekly Market Update: Tech Stocks Lead Market Lower, Basic Materials Gain | Morningstar

Tech stocks led declines, while basic materials gained, reflecting a sector rotation in early 2026. The technology sector underperformed, and basic materials surged, driven by AI demand. This shift indicates a fundamental change in market dynamics.

#### Weekly Market Wrap: Stocks Extend 2025 Rally as Basic Materials, Tech Rise Frank Lee Dec 26, 2025 [...] ## Top Stock Gainers

Tilray Brands TLRY, EchoStar SATS, Cogent Communications CCOI, AmeriCold Logistics COLD, and Uniti Group UNIT were the top performers among US-listed stocks covered by Morningstar analysts. [...] Alnylam rounds out the list with a 13.22% decline this week. This 2-star, narrow-moat company has decreased 11.94% over the past three months but rose 62.36% over the past 12 months. The stock closed the week at $397.55, trading at a 33% premium to to its fair value estimate of $310 per share.

U.S. equities have led since bottoming on March 30, return-ing to net positive territory as investors see the conflict as unlikely to derail economic and earnings growth. The AI theme is back as a primary driver, with tech, communica-tion services and consumer discretionary leading gains.
Notably, these sectors also drove much of the initial draw-down due to previously elevated valuations. Emerging market equities have also turned net positive with AI-linked strength in Asia, while developed ex-U.S. equities remain net negative amid concerns around energy sensitivity. This rotation is more than a simple market correction; it represents a fundamental shift in industry trends from digital speculation to physical necessity. Historically, when Tech valuations reach these extremes, a reversion to the mean is inevitable. However, the current shift is unique because it is tied to the physical constraints of the AI revolution. The demand for power, cooling, and raw materials has turned Basic Materials into a strategic "picks and shovels" play for the very technology that is currently lagging. This creates a ripple effect where traditional mining and industrial companies are now viewed as essential components of the high-tech supply chain. [...] The opening weeks of 2026 have been characterized by a stark divergence in sector performance. While the Technology sector has struggled, falling 0.40% year-to-date, Basic Materials has surged by 9.05%, and Consumer Defensives have climbed a healthy 5.9%. This rotation was catalyzed by a convergence of "AI bubble fatigue" and a shifting macroeconomic reality. Following three consecutive interest rate cuts in late 2025 by the Federal Reserve, which brought the Federal Funds Rate to a range of 3.50%–3.75%, the anticipated "tech melt-up" initially occurred but quickly exhausted itself as investors began to demand proof of operational efficiency and margin expansion. [...] Photo for article

As the third week of January 2026 draws to a close, the financial landscape is witnessing a profound structural shift that many analysts are calling "The Great Realignment." After two years of nearly unrivaled dominance by Silicon Valley’s elite, the momentum that once fueled the high-flying Technology sector has hit a valuation ceiling. In its place, investors are flocking to "Old Economy" stalwarts, with the Basic Materials and Consumer Defensives sectors significantly outpacing the broader market in a historic rotation of capital.

responsible for the formatting or configuration of these materials or for any inaccuracy in T. Rowe Price’s presentation thereof. [...] ## U.S.

Most major U.S. stock indexes finished the week higher, with several hitting record highs, as some generally positive economic data, ongoing strength in artificial intelligence (AI)-linked stocks, and upbeat earnings results helped offset continued uncertainty surrounding the U.S.-Iran conflict. The technology-heavy Nasdaq Composite led gains, followed by the S&P 500 and Russell 2000 Indexes. The Dow Jones Industrial Average declined.

Equities began the week on a cautious note following a strong three-week rally as hopes for near-term de-escalation in the Middle East faded. However, stocks rebounded on Wednesday after a ceasefire extension helped ease concerns, although heightened geopolitical uncertainty persisted through the end of the week.

content.outlook: Looking ahead, There have been some small downgrades to earnings expectations for this year in the face of higher oil prices, with these adjustments, as we might anticipate, showing up most in the consumer discretionary and industrial sectors. However, earnings growth estimates in these sectors remain solid, and a. Market participants will continue to monitor developments closely for signals about the direction of sector performance trends and their broader implications for portfolio allocation and risk management strategies.
